title: (PDF) 2007 The Mystery of the Bayon Face Towers - Academia.edu
highlights: essay examines the cultural and religious shifts in the Bàyon temple complex during and after the reign of Jayavarma Parameśvara, focusing on the iconography of the face towers. It questions the interpretations of the faces as Buddhist or Shaiva and discusses evidence of religious tolerance and the alterations made post-Jayavarman VII. The findings suggest a complex interplay between Buddhism and Hinduism through the periods, highlighting the challenges of correctly identifying the temple's original devotional practices.  ... Currently, we are working on a 3D digital-archiving project of the Bayon Temple. It is a building of stonework that was built in the 12th century in Cambodia. It is famous for its towers with four faces at the four cardinal points. According to research by JSA (Japanese government team for Safeguarding Angkor), the faces can be classified into three groups based on subjective criteria. title: The Bayon's many faces - Heritage Vietnam Airlines
highlights: Bayon in moonlight, the time I’ve spent there, admiring a temple with  ...    The term “Bayon” refers to the most distinctive temple in the complex, the carved faces in the temple, and this particular architectural style. The carved faces gracing the main towers and gates are hotly debated by scholars. Some identify them as the many faces of the Hindu god Shiva. Others believe they represent King Jayavarman VII, who began building the complex. The most popular theory is also the most reasonable: the Bayon are the faces of Bodhisattvas. The statues seem to display a full understanding of and willingness to share the burdens of earthly life. Bodhisattvas,  ...    Scholarly debates about origins and symbolism still rage today. However, one fact captured my interest: regardless of  ...    intersection of cultures. title: The Bayon: A temple with many faces - Smarthistory
highlights: The mystery over the temple’s name contributes to the enigmatic face-towers that define the temple’s architecture. Since the Bayon’s rediscovery by French scholars and explorers in the 19th century, the identity of the smiling figures that greet visitors to the temple and to the city of Angkor Thom has been debated. Do they depict Buddhist or Hindu deities, or do they depict King Jayavarman VII smiling over his empire? Inscriptions from the temple along with its name could aid in our understanding of the identity of the four-faced figures; however, it has been presumed by scholars that such inscriptions were destroyed by successive rulers who took over Angkor Thom after the death of  ...    crown, and his elongated earlobes are adorned with inverted lotus jeweled earrings. According to Angkor-scholar Maurice Glaize, the Bayon appears "as but a muddle of stones, a sort of moving chaos assaulting the sky." ### Buddhist symbolism in the foundation of the temple by King Jayavarman VII According to scholars, King Jayavarman VII bears a strong resemblance to the face towers of the Bayon.  ...    From the vantage point of the temple's upper terrace, one is struck by "the serenity of the stone faces" occupying many towers. The similarity of the 216 gigantic faces on the temple's towers to other statues of the has led many scholars to the conclusion that the faces are representations of Jayavarman VII, himself.
